query: effectiveness of golodirsen in treating Duchenne muscular dystrophy

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmaceutical Interventions
retrieved_docs.content: Golodirsen (SRP-4053), an antisense therapy designed to treat Duchenne muscular dystrophy, is approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). However, the evidence supporting its use, particularly in cases requiring exon 53 skipping, is still evolving and not fully established. [197] [198]

retrieved_docs.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Novel Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Gene therapies include medications that bind RNA and skip over the defective codon. This produces a shorter but potentially functional protein. Eteplirsen is an exon 51 skipping antisense oligonucleotide medication used for this purpose. Eteplirsen has been approved by the FDA for this purpose.

retrieved_docs.title: Rhabdomyolysis -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Gene therapy is being explored for treating inherited muscular dystrophies such as Duchenne and Becker. This includes various technologies such as read-through of early stop codons and exon skipping. Edasalonexent (CAT-1004), a nuclear factor κB (NF-κB) inhibitor that combines structural elements of salicylic acid and docosahexaenoic acid, has been evaluated in a phase 3, placebo-controlled clinical trial with some reported success. ( NCT03703882 ). Vamorolone is a glucocorticoid that stabilizes membranes by reducing lipid peroxidation and inhibiting NF-kB-mediated inflammation; it has also demonstrated functional improvement of symptoms. Many other novel treatments are being studied. [20] [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Glucocorticoid Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort, an oxazoline derivative of prednisone, is sometimes preferred over prednisone as it has a better side effect profile and has an estimated dosage equivalency of 1:1.3 compared with prednisone. The recommended dosage is 0.9 mg/kg/day.

retrieved_docs.title: Dystrophinopathies -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the presentation of Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Outline the evaluation of a patient with muscular dystrophy. Summarize the treatment options for muscular dystrophy. Outline the evaluation and management of dystrophinopathies and the role of interprofessional team members in collaborating to provide well-coordinated care and enhance patient outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation therapy (RT) has been studied as a potential treatment for muscular dystrophy, particularly in the context of Duchenne muscular dystrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Cardiomyopathy
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment with angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors and/or beta-blockers is recommended. Early studies suggest that ACE inhibitors may slow the progression of the disease and prevent the onset of heart failure. Overt heart failure is treated with digoxins and diuretics, as in other patients with cardiomyopathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmaceutical Interventions
retrieved_docs.content: Steroids, specifically glucocorticoids like prednisone, administered at a dose of 0.75 mg/kg per day, significantly slow the progression of muscular dystrophy for up to 3 years. [189] While some patients may not tolerate glucocorticoid therapy due to factors like weight gain and an increased risk of fractures, recent evidence suggests that early oral steroid use can lead to markedly improved outcomes. [190] [191] This includes additional walking years for children and increased life expectancy. [71] The use of glucocorticoids in Becker dystrophy and their effectiveness in treating myotonic muscular dystrophy remain areas where research is ongoing, and evidence is not yet conclusive. [192] [193] Some individuals with facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy improve with steroid therapy, particularly when rapidly progressive weakness is observed. [194]

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, there are no ongoing clinical trials specifically evaluating the use of RT for muscular dystrophy. However, there are ongoing trials exploring gene therapy and other novel approaches for the treatment of DMD, which may have implications for the use of RT in the future.

retrieved_docs.title: Becker Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Therapy of Muscle Dystrophy
retrieved_docs.content: There is no approved BMD treatment to date. However, the management approach to this condition is often similar to that for milder DMD cases. Corticosteroids have been the mainstay of treatment for patients with significant muscle weakness. Prednisolone at a dose of 0.75 mg/kg/day or deflazacort at a dose of 0.9 mg/kg/day may be given initially. [26] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: No medical cure exists for this congenital dystrophy, and the disease has a poor prognosis. Treatment is centered on glucocorticoid therapy, prevention of contractures, and medical care of cardiomyopathy and respiratory compromise. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Glucocorticoid Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoid therapy decreases the rate of apoptosis of myotubes and can decelerate myofiber necrosis. Prednisone is used in patients four years and older in whom muscle function is declining or plateauing. Prednisone is recommended at a dosage of (0.75 mg/kg per day or 10 mg/kg per week is given over two weekend days).

retrieved_docs.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Glucocorticoid Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Studies have shown that glucocorticoid treatment is associated with improved pulmonary function, delayed development of scoliosis, reduced incidence and progression of cardiomyopathy, and overall improved mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Another study published in the journal Frontiers in Oncology in 2019 reviewed the available literature on the use of RT for muscular dystrophy. The authors found that while there is limited data on the effectiveness of RT for muscular dystrophy, early studies suggest that low-dose RT may be a promising treatment option. However, more research is needed to determine the optimal dose and timing of RT for this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Dystrophinopathies -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: To date, there are several therapies under investigation, from gene repair and therapy to drugs affecting the expression of dystrophin. Eteplirsen is one of the latter. Approved by the FDA for infusion in 2016, it works by skipping exon 51 during pre-mRNA splicing correcting dystrophin expression. [21] Ataluren is another drug on trials, with the goal of bypassing pathogenic variants through promoting ribosomal read-through, therefore continuing dystrophin expression. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Becker Muscular Dystrophy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacists: ensure that patients on glucocorticoids have the right dose and are aware of these drugs' possible side effects.

Beyond antirheumatic agents, several drugs are listed as members of “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system in ATC.” These include Palovarotene, a retinoic‑acid‑receptor agonist used for skeletal disorders; aceneuramic acid, a substrate‑replacement therapy for certain metabolic myopathies; and eteplirsen, an antisense oligonucleotide employed in the treatment of Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Their inclusion underscores the diversity of pharmacologic options available for musculoskeletal conditions, ranging from disease‑modifying agents to gene‑targeted therapies. Overall, the hierarchy connects anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic products to the musculoskeletal drug class, situates specific antirheumatic agents (such as gold preparations, quinolines, and penicillamine derivatives) within that framework, and places additional musculoskeletal therapies like

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1622
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] serves as a parent category that includes both nedokromil and nusinersen. Nedokromil is identified as a member of the class of antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases, excluding corticosteroids, indicating its use in respiratory conditions where allergy‑mediated bronchoconstriction is a concern. Nusinersen, while also belonging to the broader N [Preparations] group, is a distinct therapeutic agent used for the treatment of spinal muscular atrophy, reflecting a neuromuscular application rather than a respiratory one. This organization demonstrates that the N [Preparations] category encompasses a range of medications with different mechanisms and clinical indications, from inhaled antiallergic agents like nedokromil to antisense oligonucleotide therapies such as nusinersen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1659
retrieved_docs.content: Nusinersen, an antisense oligonucleotide therapy, produces a physiologic effect described as increased protein synthesis; this enhancement of protein production, particularly of the survival motor neuron (SMN) protein, underlies its therapeutic benefit in disorders such as spinal muscular atrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 569
retrieved_docs.content: Aceneuramic acid, eteplirsen, and nusinersen are all classified within the ATC group “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system,” indicating that each is used to address conditions affecting the musculo‑skeletal system. Nusinersen’s therapeutic action is characterized by an increase in protein synthesis, a physiologic effect that contributes to its clinical benefit in these disorders. The shared classification of these three agents underscores their common relevance to musculo‑skeletal disease management, while the distinct mechanism of increased protein synthesis by nusinersen highlights the importance of understanding individual drug actions for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1606
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen has a physiologic effect of decreasing protein synthesis, meaning that treatment with this antisense oligonucleotide leads to reduced synthesis of proteins in the body. This decrease in protein synthesis is a key aspect of mipomersen’s mechanism of action and contributes to its therapeutic and safety profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1912
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] is a categorical grouping of pharmaceutical agents that encompasses both nedokromil and nusinersen. Nedokromil is a mast‑cell stabilizer employed in the management of allergic airway diseases such as asthma, while nusinersen is an antisense oligonucleotide therapy indicated for spinal muscular atrophy. By classifying these distinct drugs under the umbrella of N [Preparations], the relationship highlights that they share a common designation as specialized medical preparations despite their differing mechanisms of action and therapeutic targets. This categorization aids clinicians and pharmacists in organizing and referencing these agents within broader drug formularies and treatment protocols.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1076
retrieved_docs.content: Antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases, excluding corticosteroids are classified within the nedokromil group, indicating that they share the pharmacological characteristics of nedokromil‑type agents. The broader category labeled N [Preparations] serves as a parent classification for both nedokromil and nusinersen, linking these two distinct drug families under a common preparatory grouping. Nusinersen, like palovarotene, aceneuramic acid, and eteplirsen, is assigned to the ATC class “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system,” reflecting its therapeutic focus on musculo‑skeletal conditions despite its primary use in spinal muscular atrophy. Palovarotene, aceneuramic acid, and eteplirsen are similarly grouped in this ATC category, underscoring their shared role in treating rare or genetic musculo‑skeletal disorders. Together, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical organization of medications: specific therapeutic agents (e.g., antiallergic inhalants, nusinersen, palovarotene, aceneuramic acid, eteplirsen) are nested within broader drug classes (nedokromil, other musculo‑skeletal system drugs) that are themselves linked to the overarching preparation category N [Preparations]. This structured classification aids clinicians in understanding drug mechanisms, therapeutic indications, and the systematic grouping of pharmacologic agents for effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 368
retrieved_docs.content: The category N [Preparations] serves as a parent grouping for several distinct agents, including the antiallergic inhalant nedokromil and the antisense oligonucleotide therapy nusinersen. Nedokromil is classified as an antiallergic inhalant for obstructive airway diseases (excluding corticosteroids) and is chemically a member of the Carboxylic acid and/or carboxylic acid derivative‑containing product class. Tilidin and mupirocin are also members of this same carboxylic‑acid‑derivative class, indicating a shared structural foundation despite their different therapeutic indications. Both mupirocin (a topical antibiotic) and nusinersen (a treatment for spinal muscular atrophy) produce the physiologic effect of Increased Protein Synthesis, illustrating that agents with diverse clinical uses can converge on similar biological outcomes. Recognizing these chemical and functional relationships helps clinicians understand drug classification, anticipate potential cross‑effects, and optimize patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.content: oligonucleotide therapy to its broader preparation group. Understanding these classifications, mechanisms (direct versus peripheral action), chemical families, formulation details, and safety relationships is essential for clinicians to select appropriate agents, anticipate therapeutic effects, and manage potential side effects in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 671
retrieved_docs.content: Palovarotene, aceneuramic acid, eteplirsen and nusinersen are all classified as members of the ATC group “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system.” In addition, Palovarotene belongs to the chemical class of retinoidy, indicating it is a retinoid‑type compound. N [Preparations] is identified as the parent category for nusinersen, meaning that nusinersen is a specific preparation derived from the broader N [Preparations] group. These hierarchical and categorical relationships provide clinicians and researchers with a clear framework for understanding how these agents are grouped pharmacologically, chemically, and therapeutically, which aids in appropriate drug selection, safety monitoring, and further development within musculo‑skeletal disorder treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1011
retrieved_docs.content: Nusinersen, mupirocin, aceneuramic acid, and eteplirsen are pharmacologic agents that share certain classifications and effects. Both mupirocin and nusinersen produce the physiologic effect of increased protein synthesis, indicating that these drugs can stimulate cellular protein production. Nusinersen is categorized within the ATC group “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system,” a classification that also includes aceneuramic acid and eteplirsen, reflecting their therapeutic focus on musculo‑skeletal conditions. Additionally, nusinersen is listed as a child preparation under the broader “N [Preparations]” category, denoting its placement within a larger formulation hierarchy. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians recognize that, while mupirocin is primarily used as a topical antibacterial, it shares a mechanistic effect with nusinersen, and that nusinersen, aceneuramic acid, and eteplirsen are grouped together for musculo‑skeletal disorder treatment, facilitating coordinated therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1373
retrieved_docs.content: Enzyme Replacements/Modifiers [TC] is a therapeutic class that encompasses the agents alglucerase and hyaluronoglukosaminidasa, both of which function as enzyme replacement or modifying drugs used to address diseases caused by deficient or dysfunctional enzymes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 866
retrieved_docs.content: Palovarotene, aceneuramic acid, and eteplirsen are all categorized within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system,” linking them as therapeutic agents used for various skeletal and muscular conditions that do not fit into more specific subclasses. In addition to this shared classification, Palovarotene is distinguished from the broader class of retinoids; it is considered to lie outside the conventional retinoid family despite some structural similarity, indicating that its mechanism of action in musculo‑skeletal disorders differs from typical retinoid activity. This distinction underscores Palovarotene’s unique pharmacologic profile while aceneuramic acid and eteplirsen remain defined solely by their role in musculo‑skeletal therapy, aiding clinicians in selecting appropriate treatments and understanding the specific relationships among these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1137
retrieved_docs.content: Aceneuramic acid, eteplirsen, and nusinersen are all classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system.” This categorization indicates that each of these agents is recognized as a therapeutic product intended to address conditions affecting the musculo‑skeletal system, and they are grouped together within the same ATC subclass despite their distinct molecular identities and mechanisms of action.

Vitamin K is identified as a type of fat‑soluble vitamin, reflecting its classification among vitamins that require lipid carriers for absorption. Although a narrower relationship was erroneously noted between retinoids (“retinoidy”) and vitamin K, the correct interpretation is that retinoids constitute a separate class of fat‑soluble vitamins (related to vitamin A) and are not subcategories of vitamin K. Palovarotene, aceneuramic acid, and eteplirsen are each members of the ATC group “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system,” indicating their therapeutic use in conditions such as rare bone disorders or muscular dystrophies. Palovarotene is also associated with the retinoid class, confirming that it functions as a retinoid‑type agent. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 821
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the knowledge network depicts a hierarchical and interrelated system in which exercise‑based therapies (المعالجة بالتمارين) serve as a foundational parent for various specialized modalities, including ultrasound‑based interventions (المعالجة بموجات فوق صوتية) and broader rehabilitative approaches (المعالجة التأهيلية). These modalities are applied across disorder‑specific therapies—such as connective tissue disorder therapy and musculoskeletal disorder therapy—demonstrating an integrated therapeutic paradigm that leverages both physical exercise and advanced energy‑based treatments to optimize patient care.
